title: Minimize the admission of privileged containers

input_row.title: Minimize the admission of privileged containers
input_row.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the securityContext.privileged flag set to true .
input_row.rationale: Privileged containers have access to all Linux Kernel capabilities and devices. A container running with full privileges can do almost everything that the host can do. This flag exists to allow special use-cases, like manipulating the network stack and accessing devices. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit privileged containers. If you need to run privileged containers, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy.
input_row.audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of privileged containers.
input_row.remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of privileged containers.
input_row.impact: Pods defined with spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged: true , spec.initContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true and spec.ephemeralContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true will not be permitted.
input_row.default_value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of privileged containers.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit mentions listing policies in use for each namespace, which can be verified through Kubernetes APIs.
- The control focuses on the admission of privileged containers, which is a standard Kubernetes security property that can be queried.
- Kubernetes has built-in mechanisms to restrict privileged containers through admission controllers and Pod Security Standards.
- The requirement to ensure that privileged containers are not admitted can be checked via the Pod Security Admission or other admission controllers.

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_pod_security_privileged_containers_restricted

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the Kubernetes API to list the admission controllers configured in the cluster. This can be done by checking the API server flags.
- Use `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o=jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].securityContext.privileged}'` to check for any running pods that have the `privileged` flag set to true.
- Validate the presence of Pod Security Standards or any custom admission controllers that restrict privileged containers.
- Clear Pass/Fail criteria:
  - Pass: No pods are found with `spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged: true`, and the admission controllers are configured to disallow privileged containers.
  - Fail: Any pod is found with `spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged: true`, or the admission controllers do not restrict privileged containers.
